message:
fix(indexer): guard lexical-rebuild batch fetch against unbounded grouping by checking limits per conversation, not per chunk

fetch_messages_for_lexical_rebuild_batch_chunked previously delegated
to storage.fetch_messages_for_lexical_rebuild_batch in chunks of
sql_batch_fetch_conversation_limit conversations and only checked the
max_messages / max_content_bytes guardrails between chunks.

Two failure modes:

  1. A single fat conversation could blow past max_content_bytes in
     one chunk read because the limit was checked AFTER the whole
     chunk came back. The per-conversation content scan saw the
     blow-up only after the work was already done.

  2. The chunked batch path dropped the per-conversation grouping
     fidelity needed by callers downstream — the batch helper
     returned a flat HashMap that could lose ordering information
     when chunks were stitched back together.

Switch to the per-conversation `fetch_messages_for_lexical_rebuild`
storage call, iterating one conversation at a time, and check the
guardrails immediately after each conversation's messages are
appended. If either limit is exceeded the function bails with a
specific anyhow!() naming the field, the actual count, the limit,
and the total conversations being processed — much easier to triage
than "batch fetch failed".

The `sql_batch_fetch_conversation_limit` argument is preserved on the
function signature for now (with a `let _ = sql_batch_fetch_conversation_limit;`
to acknowledge the lint) so external callers that compute and pass
it don't break — but it's no longer used internally. Will retire the
argument in a follow-up once those callers are audited.

Trade-off: per-conversation fetches incur more SQL round-trips than
the batched path, but the lexical-rebuild path is already
throughput-bounded by the embedder downstream, not by SQL latency,
and the predictable bounded-memory behavior is worth the extra
round-trips.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
